main_summary.summary: Grace Hall Miller, born July 21, 1932 in Baker County, Georgia, was interviewed March 9, 2013 at Albany State University by Will Griffin for the Civil Rights History Project. The sixth and youngest child of farmer Joe Nathan Hall and homemaker Nanny Hall, Miller secretly married Hosie Miller Sr. in 1946 at age 14 at the probate court; they had five daughters and one son and farmed in Baker County for eighteen years. In 1958 Grace and Hosie Miller became the first two Black voters to register in Baker County; that same period saw bombings of the cars of Mr. Cunningham and Mr. Ackie King and the framing of Mr. Cunningham with planted stomp whiskey by Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson. In March 1965 Hosie Miller was shot in the back by a white relative in a dispute over a cow that had strayed into their pasture; he died after a week, leaving Grace pregnant with their son Hosie Jr., born that June. After her husband's killing the Miller home became a base for the Baker County movement; Charles Sherrod of SNCC came in and later married Miller's oldest daughter Shirley. Miller was elected to the Baker County Board of Education and served until 2013. She describes a cross-burning at her home, federal Justice Department intervention in voter registration, and the 2010 USDA controversy over Shirley Sherrod.
key_themes: widowhood and family resilience after a racially motivated killing
Black women in local elected office
main_summary.historical_significance:
Miller's testimony documents the Baker County, Georgia, freedom struggle from the perspective of a woman whose family was among the first to register and whose husband was killed by a white neighbor in 1965. The interview supplies first-person detail on the 1958 bombings of activists' cars and homes (Joshua Williams, Mr. Cunningham, Mr. Ackie King), the local enforcement role of Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson, Justice Department intervention against changed registration forms, and the use of the Miller home as a SNCC base where Charles Sherrod organized.

The interview also provides family-side context for two figures in the national record: Shirley Sherrod, Miller's oldest daughter and later USDA Georgia state director of rural development, whose 2010 firing Miller recounts from a mother's vantage; and the Spooner family rescue story that Shirley Sherrod referenced in her NAACP speech. Miller's narration links 1950s Baker County voter-registration violence, 1965 racial murder, late-1960s school integration, and twenty-first-century school-board politics within one continuous local biography.

chapters.title: The Baby of Six in Baker County
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Will Griffin at Albany State University in March 2013, Grace Hall Miller gives her name and her July 1932 birth as the sixth and youngest child of farmer Joe Nathan Hall and housewife Nanny Hall in lower Baker County, and tells of marrying Hosie Miller Sr. secretly in 1946 while still in school.

chapters.title: Whatever She Does Is Fine With Me
chapters.summary: She recalls her parents' upset that their baby daughter, who could not yet cook or wash, had married, Hosie reassuring them he would do the cooking, and the family they built of five girls and one boy with a father so devoted the daughters claimed him.

chapters.title: If You Want Something, Ask for It
chapters.summary: Miller remembers her parents as church workers, her father a deacon who taught her never to steal and to treat others as she would be treated, and her early walk school two miles from home where she begged her father for firewood while bused white children threw spitballs at the walkers.

chapters.title: Newton Colored High and the Teachers Who Lasted
chapters.summary: She describes going to Newton Colored High School while her husband's family drove their children to Mitchell County for schooling, names dedicated teachers like Miss Granberry and Miss Smith and a principal named Bradley, and laments that schools no longer teach the homemaking, Bible verses, and life skills that stayed with her.

chapters.title: Married at Fourteen at the Probate
chapters.summary: Miller tells how Hosie courted her at church and BTU, married her at the probate office at fourteen without her parents' knowledge until his brother revealed it, and teased her gently through her early cooking failures while they cleared every disagreement before bed.

chapters.title: He Painted the Room Blue
chapters.summary: She recounts their eighteen years together, Hosie's wish that no boy be good enough for his daughters, and how after he was killed she waited and at her dying mother's urging tried once more for the boy he had so badly wanted, a son born that June whom she was allowed to name only Hosie.

chapters.title: He Shot Him in the Back Over a Cow
chapters.summary: Miller describes the March morning Hosie went with a man to retrieve a cow, the dispute when the man claimed Hosie's cow as his own, and how the man, George Williamson, shot Hosie in the back with buckshot, after which he lingered a week before dying at six in the morning as she dressed to visit him.

chapters.title: They Wouldn't Even Indict Him
chapters.summary: Left with the children and a baby three weeks old when the movement began, Miller tells how the whole community sat on her lawn to help her heal, and how the all-white jury of Baker County men who knew the killer would not indict him across three attempts, the killer being her own father's brother whom she grew up around.

chapters.title: The First Two to Register
chapters.summary: Miller recalls how she and Hosie became the first two people to register to vote in Baker County while paying taxes, her sister Josephine vowing to follow, the registrar too nervous to manage it, and how the county switched the short citizenship card for a long literacy sheet until the Justice Department forced a change.

chapters.title: They Bombed the Old Men's Houses
chapters.summary: She names elders who tried to register and were attacked the same night, including her uncle Joshua Williams in his eighties, Mr. Cunningham framed with planted moonshine whiskey and jailed two weeks, and others whose cars were bombed, recalling the feared local figure they called Gator Johnson.

chapters.title: Pumping the Gun on the Porch
chapters.summary: After Hosie's death the movement made her new house a meeting place and Charles Sherrod came in, drawing threats that she was violating her farm-home loan and a cross burned in her yard, and Miller tells how she handed guns to her daughters and pumped a shotgun on the porch until neighbors and her brothers drove the carloads off under gunfire, after which the movement men, summoned by an ordinary phone, watched the house nightly for two months so her frightened children could sleep.

chapters.title: They Really Caught It in School
chapters.summary: She describes her children among the first to integrate the local school, blocked at first by state patrol, taunted by teachers and classmates who twisted the word so they could still say it, and an incident where her daughter was hauled to the superintendent's office over a white girl's claim, after which Miller confronted two white officials and the harassment stopped.

chapters.title: Sherrod Married My Oldest Daughter
chapters.topic: SNCC's Stay and a Run for Office
chapters.summary: Miller says SNCC stayed in Baker County about two years after Sherrod arrived, that he sent her oldest daughter to college and married her, and that her daughter's treatment at the superintendent's office moved her to ask a mass meeting in Springfield for support and pay the thirty-five-dollar fee to run for the board of education.

chapters.title: I Picked Peas to Get the Vote
chapters.summary: She recounts discovering she had filed in the wrong district yet refusing her money back, winning a runoff against two white men, campaigning door to door by shelling peas and picking butter beans alongside voters on a platform of quality education, and the one man who called her a name yet voted for her, and Roy Kelly, whose lost billfold her father had once returned.

chapters.title: I Had to Listen and to Learn
chapters.topic: Working the Board and Its Fights
chapters.summary: Miller describes earning the respect of the white board members by listening and refusing to be bullied while keeping her focus on Baker County's children, and her present alarm that an all-white board is pushing out a capable Black superintendent before his June contract ends and the CRCT testing, ignoring his agenda in a school district that is ninety percent Black.

chapters.part: Shirley Sherrod and a Family's History
chapters.title: Just Like the Night They Killed My Husband
chapters.summary: Miller recalls hearing of her daughter Shirley Sherrod's forced resignation from the Department of Agriculture, comparing the relatives shielding her from the news to the night Hosie was killed, driving over crying, and her certainty that she had raised no fool when officials stopped Shirley on the highway and would not listen to the full tape.

chapters.part: Shirley Sherrod and a Family's History
chapters.title: If You Don't Know Your History
chapters.topic: Teaching the Young and a Legacy
chapters.summary: She worries that younger people do not know the segregation she lived through, the colored water fountains and separate doctors' rooms, and urges that children be taught their history, then reflects on turning eighty and on the school road named Grace Miller Circle and the road by her house named Grace Miller Road, honors given while she is alive to smell the flowers.

chapters.part: Shirley Sherrod and a Family's History
chapters.title: They Own That Farm Now
chapters.summary: Miller closes on Shirley as Georgia's first Black state director of rural development, her belief the episode was a setup sprung from an NAACP speech, and the story Shirley told of forgiving her own father's killing and helping the Spooner family, who reached CNN to clear her and who, with help found at the last minute, kept and still own their farm.

loc_video.caption: Grace Miller oral history interview conducted by Will Griffin in Albany, Georgia, 2013 March 09
